title: Making pdp faster
text: default, partial() calls predict() per grid point. predict() methods non-trivial per-call overhead, often much faster stack several grid points together score single call. batch.size argument specifies (approximate) maximum number rows score per call: Batching changes predictions computed, result. see , plot classic curve overlay batched results points — fall exactly curve:  trade-memory: batch.size = 1e6, million rows held memory . Pick batch size comfortably fits machine. Note batch.size requires prediction function return one prediction per row newdata, combined pred.fun aggregates predictions.
code: system.time(  # classic: one predict() call per grid point   pd1 <- partial(boston.rf, pred.var = "lstat", train = boston) ) #>    user  system elapsed  #>   0.425   0.008   0.433 system.time(  # batched: score up to one million rows per predict() call   pd2 <- partial(boston.rf, pred.var = "lstat", train = boston,                  batch.size = 1e6) ) #>    user  system elapsed  #>   0.179   0.000   0.179 identical(pd1, pd2) #> [1] TRUE plot(pd1)  # classic (line) tinyplot::tinyplot_add(yhat ~ lstat, data = pd2, type = "p", col = "red2")

title: Making pdp faster
text: partial() can compute grid points (batches) parallel via foreach package. Register parallel backend (e.g., doParallel) set parallel = TRUE: model’s predict() method lives package, pass along via paropts (e.g., paropts = list(.packages = "ranger")) workers can find .
code: library(doParallel)  cl <- makeCluster(4)  # use 4 workers registerDoParallel(cl) pd <- partial(boston.rf, pred.var = c("lstat", "rm"), chull = TRUE,               train = boston, parallel = TRUE) stopCluster(cl)

title: Making pdp faster
text: gbm models, partial() defaults recursive = TRUE, uses Friedman’s weighted tree traversal method (implemented C++) instead brute force approach. much faster since never touch training data: Overlaying results shows two methods produce nearly curve:  small differences expected: rather averaging predictions training data, recursive method weights path tree proportion training observations followed , quite thing predictors correlated (). Note recursive method used ice = TRUE, pred.fun, inv.link.
code: library(gbm)  set.seed(103) boston.gbm <- gbm(cmedv ~ ., data = boston, distribution = "gaussian",                   n.trees = 500, interaction.depth = 3, shrinkage = 0.1)  system.time(   pd.recursive <- partial(boston.gbm, pred.var = "lstat", n.trees = 500,                           train = boston)  # recursive = TRUE is the default ) #>    user  system elapsed  #>   0.002   0.000   0.002 system.time(   pd.brute <- partial(boston.gbm, pred.var = "lstat", n.trees = 500,                       recursive = FALSE, train = boston, batch.size = 1e6) ) #>    user  system elapsed  #>   0.291   0.000   0.291 plot(pd.recursive)  # recursive (line) tinyplot::tinyplot_add(yhat ~ lstat, data = pd.brute, type = "p", col = "red2")

title: Making pdp faster
text: Finally, approx = TRUE computes much cheaper approximation partial dependence: rather averaging training data, predictions computed single “exemplar” observation (continuous predictors fixed median; categorical predictors frequent value). spirit plotmo package useful quick first look large data sets:  underlying exemplar() function also exported; can achieve thing (control) passing exemplar record train , optionally, pred.grid.
code: partial(boston.rf, pred.var = "lstat", approx = TRUE, plot = TRUE,         train = boston)

title: Individual conditional expectation (ICE) curves
text: Set ice = TRUE call partial() (ICE curves available single predictor): result contains yhat.id column identifying observation prediction belongs . plot() method draws individual curves along average (PDP) red:
code: library(pdp) library(randomForest)  data(boston) set.seed(101) boston.rf <- randomForest(cmedv ~ ., data = boston, ntree = 250)  rm.ice <- partial(boston.rf, pred.var = "rm", ice = TRUE, train = boston) head(rm.ice)  # one row per observation per grid point #>        rm     yhat yhat.id #> 1 3.56100 24.08488       1 #> 2 3.66538 24.08488       1 #> 3 3.76976 24.08488       1 #> 4 3.87414 24.08488       1 #> 5 3.97852 24.08488       1 #> 6 4.08290 24.08488       1 plot(rm.ice, alpha = 0.1, rug = TRUE, train = boston)

title: Individual conditional expectation (ICE) curves
text: curves different starting points, can hard judge whether parallel (.e., interaction). Centered ICE curves force every curve start zero, making heterogeneity shape curves much easier see. Either set center = TRUE call partial() center plotting time:  divergence curves rm roughly 6.5 indicates interaction least one predictor.
code: plot(rm.ice, center = TRUE, alpha = 0.1)

title: Individual conditional expectation (ICE) curves
text: pred.fun argument gives full control predictions generated. must function exactly two arguments—object newdata—may return single (aggregated) prediction one prediction per row newdata. Whenever multiple predictions per grid point returned, get ICE-style output automatically. example, random forest ensemble, display one curve per tree rather per observation, compute trimmed mean instead usual average:  Similarly, pred.fun natural way handle models whose predict() methods need special arguments return unusual structures; see vignette("pdp", package = "pdp") simpler built-behavior.
code: # 10% trimmed mean instead of the ordinary average pred.trimmed <- function(object, newdata) {   mean(predict(object, newdata = newdata), trim = 0.1) }  pd.trimmed <- partial(boston.rf, pred.var = "rm", pred.fun = pred.trimmed,                       train = boston) plot(pd.trimmed)

title: Individual conditional expectation (ICE) curves
text: Goldstein, ., Kapelner, ., Bleich, J., Pitkin, E. (2015). Peeking inside black box: Visualizing statistical learning plots individual conditional expectation. Journal Computational Graphical Statistics, 24(1), 44–65.

title: Introduction to pdp
text: pdp hosted r-universe:
code: # Install from r-universe (recommended): install.packages("pdp", repos = c("https://bgreenwell.r-universe.dev",                                   "https://cloud.r-project.org"))  # Install the latest development version from GitHub: pak::pak("bgreenwell/pdp")

title: Introduction to pdp
text: ’ll use Boston housing data (included pdp) random forest. partial() function needs (minimum) fitted model name predictor interest. ’s good practice also supply original training data via train argument. default partial() returns data frame, makes easy plot whatever graphics package prefer. pdp ships two plotting options: plot() — lightweight base R graphics via tinyplot; plotPartial() — lattice graphics.   can also let partial() plot directly setting plot = TRUE choosing plot.engine ("tinyplot", default, "lattice").
code: library(pdp) library(randomForest)  data(boston)  # load the (corrected) Boston housing data set.seed(101)  # for reproducibility boston.rf <- randomForest(cmedv ~ ., data = boston, ntree = 250)  # Partial dependence of cmedv on lstat pd <- partial(boston.rf, pred.var = "lstat", train = boston) head(pd) #>    lstat     yhat #> 1 1.7300 30.90476 #> 2 2.4548 30.90256 #> 3 3.1796 30.85973 #> 4 3.9044 30.39991 #> 5 4.6292 28.81125 #> 6 5.3540 26.90529 # tinyplot-based plot() method; rug marks show the min/max and deciles of # lstat to help avoid interpreting the plot where there's little data plot(pd, rug = TRUE, train = boston) # lattice-based equivalent plotPartial(pd, rug = TRUE, train = boston)

title: Introduction to pdp
text: Partial dependence extends naturally pairs predictors (plot becomes false color level plot, .e., heatmap):  chull = TRUE restricts grid convex hull training values lstat rm, reduces risk extrapolating outside region data. Factor predictors handled automatically result faceted displays.
code: pd2 <- partial(boston.rf, pred.var = c("lstat", "rm"), chull = TRUE,                train = boston) plot(pd2, contour = TRUE)

title: Introduction to pdp
text: classification models, partial dependence computed predicted probability “focus” class (first class, default; use .class change ) centered logit scale. Set prob = TRUE use probability scale instead:
code: data(pima)  # load the Pima Indians diabetes data pima2 <- na.omit(pima) set.seed(102) pima.rf <- randomForest(diabetes ~ ., data = pima2, ntree = 250)  # Partial dependence of the probability of testing positive on glucose partial(pima.rf, pred.var = "glucose", prob = TRUE, which.class = "pos",         plot = TRUE, rug = TRUE, train = pima2)

title: Introduction to pdp
text: models make predictions transformed scale (e.g., Poisson models often predict log scale). Use inv.link transform predictions back response scale partial dependence function computed:
code: fit <- glm(carb ~ ., data = mtcars, family = poisson)  # Partial dependence of the number of carburetors on mpg (response scale) partial(fit, pred.var = "mpg", inv.link = exp, plot = TRUE, train = mtcars)

title: Introduction to pdp
text: default, partial() evaluates continuous predictors equally spaced grid () 51 values spanning range. can controlled via: grid.resolution — number equally spaced grid points; quantiles = TRUE — use sample quantiles (see probs) instead, keeps grid inside bulk data; trim.outliers = TRUE — trim outliers constructing grid; pred.grid — supply exact grid values .
code: partial(boston.rf, pred.var = "lstat", quantiles = TRUE, probs = 1:19/20,         plot = TRUE, train = boston)

title: Introduction to pdp
text: vignette("ice-curves", package = "pdp") covers individual conditional expectation (ICE) curves user-supplied prediction functions. vignette("faster-pdp", package = "pdp") covers options speeding computations (e.g., batched predictions parallel processing). R Journal article “pdp: R Package Constructing Partial Dependence Plots” provides detailed introduction.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Brandon M. Greenwell. Author, maintainer.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Brandon M. Greenwell (2017). pdp: R Package Constructing Partial Dependence Plots. R Journal, 9(1), 421--436. URL https://journal.r-project.org/archive/2017/RJ-2017-016/index.html.
code: @Article{,   title = {pdp: An {R} Package for Constructing Partial Dependence Plots},   author = {Brandon M. Greenwell},   journal = {The R Journal},   year = {2017},   volume = {9},   number = {1},   pages = {421--436},   url = {https://doi.org/10.32614/RJ-2017-016},   doi = {10.32614/RJ-2017-016}, }

title: pdp — Partial Dependence Plots in R
text: pdp R package constructing partial dependence plots (PDPs) individual conditional expectation (ICE) curves. PDPs ICE curves part larger framework referred interpretable machine learning (IML), also includes (limited ) variable importance plots (VIPs). VIPs (available R package vip) help visualize feature impact (either locally globally), PDPs ICE curves help visualize feature effects. -progress, comprehensive, overview IML can found following URL: https://github.com/christophM/interpretable-ml-book. detailed introduction pdp published R Journal: “pdp: R Package Constructing Partial Dependence Plots”, https://journal.r-project.org/articles/RJ-2017-016/index.html. can track development https://github.com/bgreenwell/pdp. report bugs issues, contact main author directly submit https://github.com/bgreenwell/pdp/issues. additional documentation examples, visit package website. right now, pdp exports following functions: partial() - compute partial dependence functions individual conditional expectations (.e., objects class "partial" "ice", respectively) various fitted model objects; plotPartial() - construct lattice-based PDPs ICE curves; plot() - construct lightweight base R PDPs ICE curves via tinyplot; topPredictors() extract “important” predictors various types fitted models. see vip instead robust flexible replacement; exemplar() - construct exemplar record data frame (experimental feature may useful constructing fast, approximate feature effect plots.)

title: pdp — Partial Dependence Plots in R
text: pdp longer available CRAN due CRAN’s stringent ever-changing policies. now hosted r-universe, provides reliable alternative distributing R packages.
code: # Install the latest stable release from r-universe (recommended): install.packages("pdp", repos = c("https://bgreenwell.r-universe.dev", "https://cloud.r-project.org"))  # Or install with pak (if needed: install.packages("pak")): pak::pak("bgreenwell/pdp@main")  # latest stable release pak::pak("bgreenwell/pdp")       # development version (devel branch)

title: pdp — Partial Dependence Plots in R
text: Development happens devel branch (repository default); main branch reserved stable releases, r-universe builds package website documents. Please open pull requests devel.

title: Boston Housing Data — boston
text: Data median housing values 506 census tracts suburbs Boston 1970 census. data frame corrected version original data Harrison Rubinfeld (1978) additional spatial information. data taken directly BostonHousing2 unneeded columns (.e., name town, census tract, uncorrected median home value) removed.

title: Boston Housing Data — boston
code: data(boston)

title: Boston Housing Data — boston
text: data frame 506 rows 16 variables. lon Longitude census tract. lat Latitude census tract. cmedv Corrected median value owner-occupied homes USD 1000's crim Per capita crime rate town. zn Proportion residential land zoned lots 25,000 sq.ft. indus Proportion non-retail business acres per town. chas Charles River dummy variable (= 1 tract bounds river; 0 otherwise). nox Nitric oxides concentration (parts per 10 million). rm Average number rooms per dwelling. age Proportion owner-occupied units built prior 1940. dis Weighted distances five Boston employment centers. rad Index accessibility radial highways. tax Full-value property-tax rate per USD 10,000. ptratio Pupil-teacher ratio town. b $1000(B - 0.63)^2$ B proportion blacks town. lstat Percentage lower status population.

title: Boston Housing Data — boston
text: Harrison, D. Rubinfeld, D.L. (1978). Hedonic prices demand clean air. Journal Environmental Economics Management, 5, 81-102. Gilley, O.W., R. Kelley Pace (1996). Harrison Rubinfeld Data. Journal Environmental Economics Management, 31, 403-405. Newman, D.J. & Hettich, S. & Blake, C.L. & Merz, C.J. (1998). UCI Repository machine learning databases [http://www.ics.uci.edu/~mlearn/MLRepository.html] Irvine, CA: University California, Department Information Computer Science. Pace, R. Kelley, O.W. Gilley (1997). Using Spatial Configuration Data Improve Estimation. Journal Real Estate Finance Economics, 14, 333-340. Friedrich Leisch & Evgenia Dimitriadou (2010). mlbench: Machine Learning Benchmark Problems. R package version 2.1-1.

title: Exemplar observation — exemplar
text: Construct single "exemplar" record data frame. now, numeric columns (including "Date" objects) replaced corresponding median value non-numeric columns replaced frequent value.

title: Exemplar observation — exemplar
code: exemplar(object)  # S3 method for class 'data.frame' exemplar(object)  # S3 method for class 'matrix' exemplar(object)  # S3 method for class 'dgCMatrix' exemplar(object)

title: Exemplar observation — exemplar
text: object data frame, matrix, dgCMatrix (latter two supported xgboost).

title: Exemplar observation — exemplar
text: data frame number columns object single row.

title: Exemplar observation — exemplar
code: set.seed(1554)  # for reproducibility train <- data.frame(   x = rnorm(100),   y = sample(letters[1L:3L], size = 100, replace = TRUE,              prob = c(0.1, 0.1, 0.8)) ) exemplar(train) #>           x y #> 1 0.1322968 c

title: Partial Dependence Functions — partial
text: Compute partial dependence functions (.e., marginal effects) various model fitting objects.

title: Partial Dependence Functions — partial
code: partial(object, ...)  # Default S3 method partial(   object,   pred.var,   pred.grid,   pred.fun = NULL,   grid.resolution = NULL,   ice = FALSE,   center = FALSE,   approx = FALSE,   quantiles = FALSE,   probs = 1:9/10,   trim.outliers = FALSE,   type = c("auto", "regression", "classification"),   inv.link = NULL,   which.class = 1L,   prob = FALSE,   recursive = TRUE,   plot = FALSE,   plot.engine = c("tinyplot", "lattice"),   smooth = FALSE,   rug = FALSE,   chull = FALSE,   levelplot = TRUE,   contour = FALSE,   contour.color = "white",   alpha = 1,   train,   cats = NULL,   check.class = TRUE,   batch.size = NULL,   progress = FALSE,   parallel = FALSE,   paropts = NULL,   ... )  # S3 method for class 'model_fit' partial(object, ...)

title: Partial Dependence Functions — partial
text: object fitted model object appropriate class (e.g., "gbm", "lm", "randomForest", "train", etc.). ... Additional optional arguments passed onto predict. pred.var Character string giving names predictor variables interest. reasons computation/interpretation, include three variables. pred.grid Data frame containing joint values interest variables listed pred.var. pred.fun Optional prediction function requires two arguments: object newdata. specified, function must return single prediction vector predictions (.e., matrix data frame). Default NULL. grid.resolution Integer giving number equally spaced points use continuous variables listed pred.var pred.grid supplied. left NULL, default minimum 51 number unique data points continuous independent variables listed pred.var. ice Logical indicating whether compute individual conditional expectation (ICE) curves. Default FALSE. See Goldstein et al. (2014) details. center Logical indicating whether produce centered ICE curves (c-ICE curves). used ice = TRUE. Default FALSE. See Goldstein et al. (2014) details. approx Logical indicating whether compute faster, approximate, marginal effect plot (similar spirit plotmo package). TRUE, partial() compute predictions across predictors specified pred.var holding predictors constant ("poor man's partial dependence" function Stephen Milborrow, author plotmo, puts ). Default FALSE. Note works ice = TRUE well. WARNING: option currently experimental. Use risk. possible (arguably safer) manually passing specific "exemplar" observation train argument specifying pred.grid manually. quantiles Logical indicating whether use sample quantiles continuous predictors listed pred.var. quantiles = TRUE grid.resolution = NULL sample quantiles used generate grid joint values partial dependence computed. probs Numeric vector probabilities values [0,1]. (Values 2e-14 outside range accepted moved nearby endpoint.) Default 1:9/10 corresponds deciles predictor variables. specify quantiles use continuous predictors listed pred.var quantiles = TRUE. trim.outliers Logical indicating whether trim outliers continuous predictors listed pred.var (using simple boxplot method) generating grid joint values partial dependence computed. Default FALSE. type Character string specifying type supervised learning. Current options "auto", "regression" "classification". type = "auto" partial try extract necessary information object. inv.link Function specifying transformation applied predictions partial dependence function computed (experimental). Default NULL (.e., transformation). option intended used models allow non-Gaussian response variables (e.g., counts). models, predictions typically returned original response scale default. example, Poisson GBMs typically return predictions log scale. case setting inv.link = exp return partial dependence function response (.e., raw count) scale. .class Integer specifying column matrix predicted probabilities use "focus" class. Default use first class. used classification problems (.e., type = "classification"). prob Logical indicating whether partial dependence classification problems returned probability scale, rather centered logit. FALSE, partial dependence function scale similar logit. Default FALSE. recursive Logical indicating whether use weighted tree traversal method described Friedman (2001). applies objects inherit class "gbm". Default TRUE much faster exact brute force approach used models. (Based C++ code behind plot.gbm.) plot Logical indicating whether return data frame containing partial dependence values (FALSE) plot partial dependence function directly (TRUE). Default FALSE. See plotPartial plotting details. plot.engine Character string specifying plotting engine use whenever plot = TRUE. Options include "tinyplot" (default; lightweight base R graphics via tinyplot package) "lattice". smooth Logical indicating whether overlay LOESS smooth. Default FALSE. rug Logical indicating whether include rug display predictor axes. tick marks indicate min/max deciles predictor distributions. helps reduce risk interpreting partial dependence plot outside region data (.e., extrapolating). used plot = TRUE. Default FALSE. chull Logical indicating whether restrict values first two variables pred.var lie within convex hull training values; affects pred.grid. helps reduce risk interpreting partial dependence plot outside region data (.e., extrapolating).Default FALSE. levelplot Logical indicating whether use false color level plot (TRUE) 3-D surface (FALSE). Default TRUE. contour Logical indicating whether add contour lines level plot. used levelplot = TRUE. Default FALSE. contour.color Character string specifying color use contour lines contour = TRUE. Default "white". alpha Numeric value [0, 1] specifying opacity alpha ( useful plotting ICE/c-ICE curves). Default 1 (.e., transparency). fact, option affects ICE/c-ICE curves level plots. train optional data frame, matrix, sparse matrix containing original training data. may required depending class object. objects store copy original training data, argument required. reasons discussed , good practice always specify argument. cats Character string indicating columns train treated categorical variables. used train inherits class "matrix" "dgCMatrix". check.class Logical indicating whether make sure column pred.grid correct class, levels, etc. Default TRUE. batch.size Optional positive integer specifying (approximate) maximum number rows score per call predict. default (batch.size = NULL), partial() calls predict per grid point (.e., nrow(train) rows time). Specifying larger batch size (e.g., batch.size = 1e6) stacks multiple grid points single call predict, often substantially faster since avoids per-call overhead prediction methods, cost additional memory. Requires prediction function return one prediction per row newdata, used pred.fun aggregates predictions. Prediction names also ignored batching (.e., yhat.id always contain integer IDs). Ignored whenever recursive method used (.e., "gbm" objects recursive = TRUE). progress Logical indicating whether display text-based progress bar. Default FALSE. parallel Logical indicating whether run partial parallel using backend provided foreach package. Default FALSE. paropts List containing additional options passed onto foreach parallel = TRUE.

title: Partial Dependence Functions — partial
text: default, partial returns object class c("data.frame", "partial"). ice = TRUE center = FALSE object class c("data.frame", "ice") returned. ice = TRUE center = TRUE object class c("data.frame", "cice") returned. three classes determine behavior plotting functions automatically called whenever plot = TRUE. Specifically, plot = TRUE plot.engine = "tinyplot" (default), plot drawn directly (side effect) data frame partial dependence values returned invisibly. plot = TRUE plot.engine = "lattice", "trellis" object returned (see lattice details); "trellis" object also include additional attribute, "partial.data", containing data displayed plot.

title: Partial Dependence Functions — partial
text: cases difficult partial extract original training data object. cases error message displayed requesting user supply training data via train argument call partial. cases partial can extract required training data object, taken environment partial called. Therefore, important change training data used construct object calling partial. problem completely avoided training data passed train argument call partial. recommended call partial plot = FALSE store results. allows flexible plotting, user waste time calling partial default plot sufficient. possible retrieve last printed "trellis" object, produced plotPartial, using trellis.last.object(). ice = TRUE prediction function given pred.fun returns prediction observation newdata, result curve observation. called individual conditional expectation (ICE) curves; see Goldstein et al. (2015) ice details.

title: Partial Dependence Functions — partial
text: J. H. Friedman. Greedy function approximation: gradient boosting machine. Annals Statistics, 29: 1189-1232, 2001. Goldstein, ., Kapelner, ., Bleich, J., Pitkin, E., Peeking Inside Black Box: Visualizing Statistical Learning Plots Individual Conditional Expectation. (2014) Journal Computational Graphical Statistics, 24(1): 44-65, 2015.

title: Partial Dependence Functions — partial
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # # Regression example (requires randomForest package to run) #  # Fit a random forest to the boston housing data library(randomForest) data (boston)  # load the boston housing data set.seed(101)  # for reproducibility boston.rf <- randomForest(cmedv ~ ., data = boston)  # Using randomForest's partialPlot function partialPlot(boston.rf, pred.data = boston, x.var = "lstat")  # Using pdp's partial function head(partial(boston.rf, pred.var = "lstat"))  # returns a data frame partial(boston.rf, pred.var = "lstat", plot = TRUE, rug = TRUE)  # The partial function allows for multiple predictors partial(boston.rf, pred.var = c("lstat", "rm"), grid.resolution = 40,         plot = TRUE, chull = TRUE, progress = TRUE)  # The plotPartial function offers more flexible plotting pd <- partial(boston.rf, pred.var = c("lstat", "rm"), grid.resolution = 40) plotPartial(pd, levelplot = FALSE, zlab = "cmedv", drape = TRUE,             colorkey = FALSE, screen = list(z = -20, x = -60))  # The plot method can be used to produce lightweight base R graphics via # the tinyplot package plot(pd, contour = TRUE)  # # Individual conditional expectation (ICE) curves #  # Use partial to obtain ICE/c-ICE curves rm.ice <- partial(boston.rf, pred.var = "rm", ice = TRUE) plotPartial(rm.ice, rug = TRUE, train = boston, alpha = 0.2) plot(rm.ice, center = TRUE, alpha = 0.2, rug = TRUE, train = boston)  # # Classification example (requires randomForest package to run) #  # Fit a random forest to the Pima Indians diabetes data data (pima)  # load the Pima Indians diabetes data set.seed(102)  # for reproducibility pima.rf <- randomForest(diabetes ~ ., data = pima, na.action = na.omit)  # Partial dependence of positive test result on glucose (default logit scale) partial(pima.rf, pred.var = "glucose", plot = TRUE, chull = TRUE,         progress = TRUE)  # Partial dependence of positive test result on glucose (probability scale) partial(pima.rf, pred.var = "glucose", prob = TRUE, plot = TRUE,         chull = TRUE, progress = TRUE) } # }

what: pdp: A general framework for constructing partial dependence (i.e., marginal effect) plots from various types machine learning models in R. — pdp-package
title: pdp: A general framework for constructing partial dependence (i.e., marginal effect) plots from various types machine learning models in R. — pdp-package
text: Partial dependence plots (PDPs) help visualize relationship subset features (typically 1-3) response accounting average effect predictors model. particularly effective black box models like random forests support vector machines.

title: pdp: A general framework for constructing partial dependence (i.e., marginal effect) plots from various types machine learning models in R. — pdp-package
text: development version can found GitHub: https://github.com/bgreenwell/pdp. right now, pdp exports following functions: partial - construct partial dependence functions (.e., objects class "partial") various fitted model objects; plotPartial - plot partial dependence functions (.e., objects class "partial") using lattice graphics; plot - plot partial dependence functions (.e., objects class "partial") using lightweight base R graphics via tinyplot; exemplar - construct single "exemplar" record data frame.

title: pdp: A general framework for constructing partial dependence (i.e., marginal effect) plots from various types machine learning models in R. — pdp-package
text: Maintainer: Brandon M. Greenwell greenwell.brandon@gmail.com (ORCID)

title: Pima Indians Diabetes Data — pima
text: Diabetes test results collected US National Institute Diabetes Digestive Kidney Diseases population women least 21 years old, Pima Indian heritage, living near Phoenix, Arizona. data taken directly PimaIndiansDiabetes2.

title: Pima Indians Diabetes Data — pima
code: data(pima)

title: Pima Indians Diabetes Data — pima
text: data frame 768 observations 9 variables. pregnant Number times pregnant. glucose Plasma glucose concentration (glucose tolerance test). pressure Diastolic blood pressure (mm Hg). triceps Triceps skin fold thickness (mm). insulin 2-Hour serum insulin (mu U/ml). mass Body mass index (weight kg/(height m)^2). pedigree Diabetes pedigree function. age Age (years). diabetes Factor indicating diabetes test result (neg/pos).

text: Newman, D.J. & Hettich, S. & Blake, C.L. & Merz, C.J. (1998). UCI Repository machine learning databases [http://www.ics.uci.edu/~mlearn/MLRepository.html]. Irvine, CA: University California, Department Information Computer Science. Brian D. Ripley (1996), Pattern Recognition Neural Networks, Cambridge University Press, Cambridge. Grace Whaba, Chong Gu, Yuedong Wang, Richard Chappell (1995), Soft Classification .k.. Risk Estimation via Penalized Log Likelihood Smoothing Spline Analysis Variance, D. H. Wolpert (1995), Mathematics Generalization, 331-359, Addison-Wesley, Reading, MA. Friedrich Leisch & Evgenia Dimitriadou (2010). mlbench: Machine Learning Benchmark Problems. title: Plotting Partial Dependence Functions — plot.partial
text: Plot partial dependence functions (.e., marginal effects) individual conditional expectation (ICE) curves using lightweight base R graphics via tinyplot package.

title: Plotting Partial Dependence Functions — plot.partial
code: # S3 method for class 'partial' plot(   x,   center = FALSE,   plot.pdp = TRUE,   pdp.col = "red2",   pdp.lwd = 2,   pdp.lty = 1,   smooth = FALSE,   rug = FALSE,   contour = FALSE,   contour.color = "white",   train = NULL,   alpha = 1,   legend.title = "yhat",   ... )  # S3 method for class 'ice' plot(   x,   center = FALSE,   plot.pdp = TRUE,   pdp.col = "red2",   pdp.lwd = 2,   pdp.lty = 1,   rug = FALSE,   train = NULL,   alpha = 1,   ... )  # S3 method for class 'cice' plot(   x,   plot.pdp = TRUE,   pdp.col = "red2",   pdp.lwd = 2,   pdp.lty = 1,   rug = FALSE,   train = NULL,   alpha = 1,   ... )

title: Plotting Partial Dependence Functions — plot.partial
text: x object inherits class "partial", "ice", "cice"; typically result call partial. center Logical indicating whether produce centered ICE curves (c-ICE curves). useful x represents set ICE curves; see partial details. Default FALSE. plot.pdp Logical indicating whether plot partial dependence function top ICE curves. Default TRUE. pdp.col Character string specifying color use partial dependence function plot.pdp = TRUE. Default "red2". pdp.lwd Integer specifying line width use partial dependence function plot.pdp = TRUE. Default 2. See par details. pdp.lty Integer character string specifying line type use partial dependence function plot.pdp = TRUE. Default 1. See par details. smooth Logical indicating whether overlay LOESS smooth. Default FALSE. rug Logical indicating whether include rug marks (.e., min/max deciles predictor distribution) predictor axes. currently supported faceted displays (.e., partial dependence two predictors least one factor). Default FALSE. contour Logical indicating whether add contour lines false color level plot used two continuous predictors. Default FALSE. contour.color Character string specifying color use contour lines contour = TRUE. Default "white". train Data frame containing original training data. required rug = TRUE. alpha Numeric value [0, 1] specifying opacity alpha; useful plotting ICE/c-ICE curves. Default 1 (.e., transparency). legend.title Character string specifying text legend title false color level plot used two continuous predictors. Default "yhat". ... Additional optional arguments passed tinyplot (e.g., palette, main, theme).

title: Plotting Partial Dependence Functions — plot.partial
text: Draws plot side effect (invisibly) returns x.

title: Plotting Partial Dependence Functions — plot.partial
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # # Regression example (requires randomForest package to run) #  # Fit a random forest to the Boston housing data library(randomForest) data (boston)  # load the boston housing data set.seed(101)  # for reproducibility boston.rf <- randomForest(cmedv ~ ., data = boston)  # Partial dependence of cmedv on lstat pd <- partial(boston.rf, pred.var = "lstat") plot(pd, rug = TRUE, train = boston)  # Partial dependence of cmedv on lstat and rm pd2 <- partial(boston.rf, pred.var = c("lstat", "rm"), chull = TRUE) plot(pd2, contour = TRUE)  # ICE and c-ICE curves rm.ice <- partial(boston.rf, pred.var = "rm", ice = TRUE) plot(rm.ice, rug = TRUE, train = boston, alpha = 0.2) plot(rm.ice, center = TRUE, alpha = 0.2) } # }

title: Plotting Partial Dependence Functions — plotPartial
text: Plots partial dependence functions (.e., marginal effects) using lattice graphics.

title: Plotting Partial Dependence Functions — plotPartial
code: plotPartial(object, ...)  # S3 method for class 'ice' plotPartial(   object,   center = FALSE,   plot.pdp = TRUE,   pdp.col = "red2",   pdp.lwd = 2,   pdp.lty = 1,   rug = FALSE,   train = NULL,   ... )  # S3 method for class 'cice' plotPartial(   object,   plot.pdp = TRUE,   pdp.col = "red2",   pdp.lwd = 2,   pdp.lty = 1,   rug = FALSE,   train = NULL,   ... )  # S3 method for class 'partial' plotPartial(   object,   center = FALSE,   plot.pdp = TRUE,   pdp.col = "red2",   pdp.lwd = 2,   pdp.lty = 1,   smooth = FALSE,   rug = FALSE,   chull = FALSE,   levelplot = TRUE,   contour = FALSE,   contour.color = "white",   col.regions = NULL,   number = 4,   overlap = 0.1,   train = NULL,   ... )

title: Plotting Partial Dependence Functions — plotPartial
text: object object inherits "partial" class. ... Additional optional arguments passed onto dotplot, levelplot, xyplot, wireframe. center Logical indicating whether produce centered ICE curves (c-ICE curves). useful object represents set ICE curves; see partial details. Default FALSE. plot.pdp Logical indicating whether plot partial dependence function top ICE curves. Default TRUE. pdp.col Character string specifying color use partial dependence function plot.pdp = TRUE. Default "red". pdp.lwd Integer specifying line width use partial dependence function plot.pdp = TRUE. Default 1. See par details. pdp.lty Integer character string specifying line type use partial dependence function  plot.pdp = TRUE. Default 1. See par details. rug Logical indicating whether include rug marks predictor axes. Default FALSE. train Data frame containing original training data. required rug = TRUE chull = TRUE. smooth Logical indicating whether overlay LOESS smooth. Default FALSE. chull Logical indicating whether restrict first two variables pred.var lie within convex hull training values; affects pred.grid. Default FALSE. levelplot Logical indicating whether use false color level plot (TRUE) 3-D surface (FALSE). Default TRUE. contour Logical indicating whether add contour lines level plot. used levelplot = TRUE. Default FALSE. contour.color Character string specifying color use contour lines contour = TRUE. Default "white". col.regions Vector colors passed levelplot's col.region argument. Defaults grDevices::hcl.colors(100) (viridis color palette used past). number Integer specifying number conditional intervals use continuous panel variables. See co.intervals equal.count details. overlap fraction overlap conditioning variables. See co.intervals equal.count details.

title: Plotting Partial Dependence Functions — plotPartial
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # # Regression example (requires randomForest package to run) #  # Load required packages library(gridExtra)  # for `grid.arrange()` library(magrittr)  # for forward pipe operator `%>%` library(randomForest)  # Fit a random forest to the Boston housing data data (boston)  # load the boston housing data set.seed(101)  # for reproducibility boston.rf <- randomForest(cmedv ~ ., data = boston)  # Partial dependence of cmedv on lstat boston.rf %>%   partial(pred.var = "lstat") %>%   plotPartial(rug = TRUE, train = boston)  # Partial dependence of cmedv on lstat and rm boston.rf %>%   partial(pred.var = c("lstat", "rm"), chull = TRUE, progress = TRUE) %>%   plotPartial(contour = TRUE, legend.title = "rm")  # ICE curves and c-ICE curves age.ice <- partial(boston.rf, pred.var = "lstat", ice = TRUE) p1 <- plotPartial(age.ice, alpha = 0.1) p2 <- plotPartial(age.ice, center = TRUE, alpha = 0.1) grid.arrange(p1, p2, ncol = 2) } # }

title: Extract Most
text: Extract "important" predictors regression classification models.

title: Extract Most
code: topPredictors(object, n = 1L, ...)  # Default S3 method topPredictors(object, n = 1L, ...)  # S3 method for class 'train' topPredictors(object, n = 1L, ...)

title: Extract Most
text: object fitted model object appropriate class (e.g., "gbm", "lm", "randomForest", etc.). n Integer specifying number predictors return. Default 1 meaning return single important predictor. ... Additional optional arguments passed onto varImp.

title: Extract Most
text: function uses generic function varImp calculate variable importance scores predictor. , sorted names n highest scoring predictors returned.

title: Extract Most
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # # Regression example (requires randomForest package to run) #  # Load required packages library(randomForest)  # Fit a random forest to the mtcars dataset data(mtcars, package = "datasets") set.seed(101) mtcars.rf <- randomForest(mpg ~ ., data = mtcars, mtry = 5, importance = TRUE)  # Top four predictors top4 <- topPredictors(mtcars.rf, n = 4)  # Construct partial dependence functions for top four predictors pd <- NULL for (i in top4) {   tmp <- partial(mtcars.rf, pred.var = i)   names(tmp) <- c("x", "y")   pd <- rbind(pd,  cbind(tmp, predictor = i)) }  # Display partial dependence functions tinyplot::tinyplot(y ~ x, facet = ~ predictor, data = pd, type = "l",                    facet.args = list(free = TRUE), ylab = "mpg")  } # }

title: Retrieve the last trellis object — trellis.last.object
code: trellis.last.object(..., prefix)

title: pdp 0.9.0
text: ggplot2-based autoplot() methods removed favor new, lightweight base R plot() methods built tinyplot. Consequently, partial()’s plot.engine argument now accepts "tinyplot" place "ggplot2", pdp longer depends ggplot2 rlang. "tinyplot" now default plotting engine partial(..., plot = TRUE); note draws plot directly (side effect) returns data frame partial dependence values invisibly. Use plot.engine = "lattice" previous behavior (.e., returned "trellis" object).

title: pdp 0.9.0
text: Fixed serious bug recursive (.e., weighted tree traversal) method "gbm" objects produced incorrect partial dependence values factor predictors; factor levels passed underlying C++ routine using 1-based (rather 0-based) integer codes, shifting level’s value reading bounds last level. partial() longer errors "gbm" objects fit list-valued distributions (e.g., distribution = list(name = "quantile", alpha = 0.5)). convex hull displayed plotPartial(chull = TRUE) now correct whenever training data contain missing values. Conflicting grid options (e.g., specifying grid.resolution quantiles) now always throw error, even predictors listed pred.var factors. Removed stray "FALSE" appended message displayed requesting progress bar recursive method.

title: pdp 0.9.0
text: New tinyplot-based plot() methods "partial", "ice", "cice" objects; see ?plot.partial details examples. New batch.size argument partial() scoring multiple grid points per call predict() (.e., batching); often substantially faster default one-call-per-grid-point approach cost additional memory. example, try batch.size = 1e6 score (roughly) one million rows per call.

title: pdp 0.9.0
text: foreach moved Imports Suggests; now required parallel execution (.e., whenever parallel = TRUE). Removed unused packages Suggests (keras, reticulate, dplyr). topPredictors() (deprecated) now throws informative error caret installed. Internal cleanup: brute force method (pardep()) consolidated four nearly identical loops one, prediction wrappers get_predictions.R now share common helper. Fixed failing tests due changes xgboost package.

title: pdp 0.8.2
text: CRAN release: 2024-10-28

title: pdp 0.8.2
text: Set R_NO_REMAP per changes CRAN policies. Updated package documentation since @docType "package" deprecated. Removed unnecessary get_training_data.Rd file cleaned arguments. Update citation file use c() instead personList() bibentry() instead citEntry().

title: pdp 0.8.1
text: CRAN release: 2022-06-07

title: pdp 0.8.1
text: Fixed ICE plot issue XGBoost models caused switching foreach package.

title: pdp 0.8.1
text: Fixed couple URLs package documentation.

title: pdp 0.8.1
text: partial()’s progress argument now accepts logical defaults FALSE.

title: pdp 0.8.0
text: CRAN release: 2022-05-11

title: pdp 0.8.0
text: New (experimental) function exemplar() constructing “exemplar” record data frame matrix-like object. See ?pdp::exemplar details (#91). partial() gained new (experimental) feature via new approx argument. approx = TRUE, partial() compute predictions across predictors specified pred.var holding predictors constant (“poor man’s partial dependence” function Stephen Milborrow, author plotmo, puts ). See ?pdp::partial details.

title: pdp 0.8.0
text: Bumped R dependency R (>= 3.6.0) support use grDevices::hcl.colors() plotPartial(). Function grid.arrange() forward pipe operator %>% longer automatically imported packages gridExtra magrittr, respectively; users encouraged load manually needed. Removed palette alpha arguments plotPartial() autoplot(); latter just got absorbed ... argument. default, plotPartial()’s col.regions argument now corresponds grDevices::hcl.colors(100), defaults viridis color palette , just without dependency. topPredictors() now deprecated removed next update. Users advised use vip package instead.

title: pdp 0.8.0
text: Added support gradient boosted Cox proportional hazards models gbm. Removed dependency retired plyr package relying directly foreach package. Consequently, ICE curves (ice = TRUE) now slightly faster compute (since code refactoring avoids post-process ICE data wide long format) corresponding progress bar (progress = "text") honest. consequence, partial() function supports simple text-based progress bar (progress = "text"), options possibly added later. Removed dependency viridis; consequently, keep ‘viridis’ color palettes autoplot(), required bumping ggplot2 dependency version 3.0.0, well code tweaks hood (#106). Removed dependency mgcv switching internal C implementation mgcv’s .() function (#107). (used behind scenes whenever partial() called chull = TRUE.) "partial" now proper subclass "data.frame" (#104); thanks @RoelVerbelen pointing . Fixed bug rug = TRUE work properly xgboost models whenever calling partial() plot = TRUE. Fixed bug partial() cats argument never actually passed pred_grid() (#86). Fixed bug partial() "gbm" objects recursive = TRUE caused factors (including ordered factors) coerced characters.

title: pdp 0.8.0
text: Switched Travis-CI GitHub Actions continuous integration. Added ICEbox mlbench list suggested packages. Refactored code easier maintenance. Switched tinytest framework increased test coverage (#84). internal function get_training_data(), used (attempt ) extract fitted model’s training data whenever train specified, (hopefully) bit flexible robust certain special cases(#90). Minor bug fixes plotting functions (.e., autoplot() plotPartial()). Training data inherits class "tibble" still officially supported, shouldn’t cause many errors point . Using autoplot() factor followed numeric pred.var longer seems issue (#79).

title: pdp 0.7.0
text: CRAN release: 2018-08-27 Added support e1071::naiveBayes(), implementation standard naive Bayes classifier (#42). Fixed bug plotPartial() caused col.regions argument effect levelplot = FALSE (#58). Fixed bug categorical variables gbm models recursive = TRUE (#63). informative progress bars (estimated time completion!!) powered progress package. use, simply call partial() option progress = "progress" (#66). Added ORCiD ID author field DESCRIPTION file. Way cooler logo? partial() gained several new plotting options: plot.engine, controls plotting engine used whenever plot = TRUE (current options include "lattice" (default) "ggplot2" (#71). arguments autoplot() plotPartial() now consistent . names () helper functions changed lowerCamelCase snake_case. partial() now works (better) tibbles (#59). partial() now treats "xgb.Booster" objects objective = "reg:logistc" regression (#68). Removed use ggplot2::aes_string() autoplot() (soft deprecated ggplot2 version 3.0.0) (#73).

title: pdp 0.6.0
text: CRAN release: 2017-07-20 Properly registered native routines disabled symbol search. Fixed bug gbm models using multinomial distribution. Refactored code improve structure. partial() gained three new options: inv.link (experimental), ice, center. latter two constructing individual conditional expectation (ICE) curves centered ICE (c-ICE) curves. inv.link option transforming predictions models can use non-Gaussian distributions (e.g., glm, gbm, xgboost). Note options added convenience results (plus much ) can still obtained using flexible pred.fun argument. (#36). plotPartial() gained five new options: center, plot.pdp, pdp.col, pdp.lwd, pdp.lty; see ?plotPartial details. Fixed default y-axis label autoplot() two numeric predictors (#48). Added CITATION file. Better support neural networks nnet package. Fixed bug nnet::multinom() models binary response.

title: pdp 0.5.2
text: CRAN release: 2017-03-13 Fixed minor pandoc conversion issue README.md. Added subdirectory called tools hold figures README.md.

title: pdp 0.5.1
text: Registered native routines disabled symbol search.

title: pdp 0.5.0
text: Added support MASS::lda(), MASS::qda(), mda::mars(). New arguments quantiles, probs, trim.outliers partial. arguments make easier construct PDPs relevant range numeric predictor without specify pred.grid, especially outliers present predictors (can distort plotted relationship). train argument can now accept matrices; particular, object class "matrix" "dgCMatrix". useful, example, working XGBoost models (.e., objects class "xgb.Booster"). New logical argument prob indicating whether partial dependence values classification problems returned original probability scale, rather centered logit; details centered logit can found page 370 second edition Elements Statistical Learning. Fixed typos NEWS.md. New function autoplot automatically creating ggplot2 graphics "partial" objects.

title: pdp 0.4.0
text: CRAN release: 2017-01-07 partial() now much faster "gbm" object due call gbm::plot.gbm() whenever pred.grid explicitly given user. (gbm::plot.gbm() exploits computational shortcut involve passes training data.) New (experimental) function topPredictors() extracting names “important” predictors. make one step easier (cases) construct PDPs “important”” features fitted model. new argument, pred.fun, allows user supply prediction function. Hence, possible obtain PDPs based median, rather mean. also possible obtain PDPs classification problems probability scale. See ?partial examples. Minor bug fixes documentation tweaks.

title: pdp 0.3.0
text: CRAN release: 2016-12-18 ... argument call partial() now refers additional arguments passed onto stats::predict() rather plyr::aaply(). example, using partial() "gbm" objects require specification n.trees can now simply passed partial() via ... argument. Added following arguments partial(): progress (plyr-based progress bars), parallel (plyr/foreach-based parallel execution), paropts (list additional arguments passed onto foreach parallel = TRUE). Various bug fixes. partial() now throws informative error message pred.grid argument refers predictors original training data. column name predicted value changed "y" "yhat".

title: pdp 0.2.0
text: CRAN release: 2016-12-03 randomForest longer imported. Added support caret package (.e., objects class "train"). Added example data sets: boston (corrected Boston housing data) pima (corrected Pima Indians diabetes data). Fixed error sometimes occurred chull = TRUE causing convex hull computed. Refactored plotPartial() modular. Added gbm support non-"binomial" families`.

title: pdp 0.1.0
text: CRAN release: 2016-10-06 randomForest now imported. Added examples.

title: pdp 0.0.6
text: Fixed non canonical CRAN URL README file.

title: pdp 0.0.5
text: partial() now makes sure column pred.grid correct class, levels, etc. partial() gained new option, levelplot, defaults TRUE. original option, contour, changed now specifies whether add contour lines whenever levelplot = TRUE.

title: pdp 0.0.4
text: CRAN release: 2016-09-17 Fixed number URLs. thorough documentation.

title: pdp 0.0.2
text: Fixed couple URLs typos. Added thorough documentation. Added support C5.0, Cubist, nonlinear least squares, XGBoost models.

title: pdp 0.0.1
text: CRAN release: 2016-09-02 Initial release.
